speaker: Participant 1
text: The campus feels inspiring at first because everything is large and technical, but after a few weeks I noticed that the experience depends a lot on whether you already know where to go between classes.

speaker: Participant 2
text: For me it was mostly exciting. I liked cycling from one side to another because it made the university feel alive.

speaker: Pepper
text: You are both naming real parts of campus life. Take a moment to compare them: when does the campus feel energizing, and when does that same scale become tiring or excluding?

speaker: Participant 1
text: It becomes tiring when you have a lecture in one faculty, a project meeting somewhere else, and then you still need to find food or a quiet seat. The distances are manageable on paper, but mentally it feels like switching worlds.

speaker: Participant 2
text: I had that during rain. Suddenly the nice distance is just annoying.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Exactly, and the campus assumes you are mobile, dry, and confident. If you are new, late, carrying a prototype, or trying not to spend money, the same layout becomes much less friendly.

speaker: Participant 2
text: I did not think about carrying prototypes, but yes, design students have that issue more.

speaker: Pepper
text: That is a helpful concrete example. What other student situations should your discussion include, so the campus experience is not based only on the easiest case?

speaker: Participant 1
text: Commuters should be included because they often arrive just before class and leave quickly after. They need reliable places for short gaps, not only beautiful study spaces for an entire afternoon.

speaker: Participant 2
text: International students too, especially if they do not understand informal rules.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Yes, informal rules are a big part of it. Nobody says whether you can sit in another faculty, use a microwave, or join a table where people are already working. You learn it by guessing.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Guessing can be stressful. A small sign can change that.

speaker: Pepper
text: You are moving toward a clear theme: hidden rules. Try to turn that into one design principle. What should TU Delft make visible without overwhelming students with information?

speaker: Participant 1
text: The principle could be permission at the point of uncertainty. If a student hesitates at a door, a seating area, or a service counter, the campus should show whether it is open and for whom.

speaker: Participant 2
text: I like that phrase, permission at uncertainty.

speaker: Participant 1
text: It could apply to study rooms, food areas, workshops, and help desks. The sign does not need to be huge. It just needs to answer the social question before the student has to ask awkwardly.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Maybe it also helps staff because students ask fewer basic questions.

speaker: Participant 1
text: That is a good implementation argument. If the benefit is student comfort and lower staff interruption, facilities might support it instead of seeing it as decoration.

speaker: Pepper
text: Nice, you now have both a student benefit and an institutional benefit. To keep it realistic, choose one place on campus where this principle would matter most and explain why.

speaker: Participant 1
text: I would choose faculty entrances and ground-floor seating because that is where the first uncertainty happens. Once you are inside, you can explore, but the entrance decides whether you feel allowed to enter at all.

speaker: Participant 2
text: I would choose the library during exam weeks because pressure is highest there.

speaker: Participant 1
text: The library is important, but it already has many signs and staff presence. The hidden problem is more in smaller faculty buildings where there is no obvious person to ask.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Fair, the smaller buildings are more ambiguous.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Maybe the pilot should be three buildings with different cultures: one open and social, one technical and closed, and one service-heavy building. Then TU Delft can compare where the signs matter.

speaker: Pepper
text: That pilot idea is concrete. What evidence would show that the supportive signs are working: fewer questions, more diverse use of spaces, student self-reports, or something else?

speaker: Participant 1
text: I would combine quick student self-reports with observation. If more students from other faculties use the seating after the signs, that suggests the permission signal changed behavior.

speaker: Participant 2
text: Self-reports are easier than observation, but observation is more convincing.

speaker: Participant 1
text: We could also ask facilities staff whether students ask fewer permission questions. That is imperfect, but it captures the everyday friction we are trying to reduce.

speaker: Participant 2
text: And compare before and after, otherwise we cannot know if signs caused it.

speaker: Pepper
text: Good caution. You are strengthening the idea by asking how to test it. Try to finish with one concise proposal that includes the pilot, the students it supports, and the evidence you would collect.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Our proposal is a permission-at-uncertainty pilot in three faculty buildings, using small supportive signs at entrances, seating areas, microwaves, workshops, and help desks. It supports new, international, commuter, and cross-faculty students.

speaker: Participant 2
text: And we would measure self-reports, observed use, and staff questions before and after.

speaker: Participant 1
text: Yes, and if the pilot works, the same design language could be reused across campus without making every wall crowded with instructions.

speaker: Participant 2
text: That sounds practical and not too expensive.
